QF.id: IV Strategies Using Interest Rate Derivatives_30

question.text: What distinguishes a covered call strategy from a naked call writing strategy in the context of interest rate derivatives?

answer.text: In a covered call, the writer owns the underlying bond; in a naked call, the writer has no underlying position
A covered call has unlimited profit potential; a naked call has limited profit
A covered call requires no margin; a naked call requires margin
A covered call can only be written on government bonds; a naked call on any bond

explanation.text: **The Logic:** A covered call involves selling a call while holding the underlying bond. If the call is exercised, the writer delivers the bond they already own. A naked call has no underlying, creating potentially unlimited loss if prices surge. **The Trap:** Covered calls have LIMITED profit (capped at strike + premium - cost), not unlimited. Both require margin in practice. There is no restriction on bond type. **Cross-Exam Context:** Covered vs naked call distinction is identical in equity options (Series VIII). **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Covered calls are conservative yield-enhancement strategies. Naked calls are highly risky speculative strategies.
